A 🧵 with a summary of the key points from the article "Development in Progress"

ref: https://t.co/uLI0bvQ52g
The article critiques the current concept of progress, arguing that it is immature and ignores many negative side effects of technological advancement.
It examines several common claims of progress (e.g. increased life expectancy, reduced poverty) and argues these are often cherry-picked or misleading when examined more closely.
The invention of synthetic fertilizers (Haber-Bosch process) is used as a case study to show the complex and far-reaching negative externalities that can result from technological "progress".
Our reductionist scientific worldview fails to account for the full complexity of reality and first-person subjective experience.
We have a kind of "Stockholm syndrome" relationship with our current technological/economic system despite its flaws 🤯

Ways to mature our concept of progress:

- More prudent problem-solving approaches
- "Yellow teaming" to anticipate negative effects of new technologies
- Stronger regulation of potentially dangerous technologies
- "Synergistic design" to create solutions with multiple benefits
A call towards a more holistic, careful, and ethically-grounded approach to technological development and societal advancement that considers long-term and wide-ranging impacts

Prudent problem-solving: A step-by-step process to ensure problems are properly identified and addressed at their root causes (even though i think it will be really hard to get to the root causes sometimes, since we are talking about super complex systems anyways)

Examples of social media redesign and regenerative agriculture can be used to illustrate how these approaches could be applied to create more holistic benefit (I thought technology was evil, but in fact, is not the tool, but how it is used)
Our current path as you might have noticed will lead us to collapse, while a more mature approach could create a radically healthier, kinder, and safer world
